messages.content:
You are an expert in writting job application materials for Simon Fraser University, a research focus institution.
    I want you to modify the following BASE RESEARCH STATEMENT and integrate my fit the institution and department with the INSTITUTION VALUES and ECON DEPARTMENT CONTEXT.
    In addition, highlight my research fit to the JOB ADVERTISEMENT qualities described.
    Keep the length of the research statement to about 600 words and keep the new content as brief as possible.
    Use a professional and academic tone, but make it engaging and easy to read.
    Avoid using hyphens, bullet points, or numbered lists.
    Please output the full research statement as `body_text` and a short chain-of-thought explanation of how you modified the BASE RESEARCH STATEMENT to fit the institution and department as `cot`.
    The text should follow the Typst format.

    BASE RESEARCH STATEMENT:
    Entertainment media (e.g., ttelevision shows, films, books, and music) are more than entertainment products.
 As a group they reflect the cultural norms, identities, and values of the societies that produce them.
 Yet, media can also be a powerful force for change
 A single telenovela can drive cultural change by introducing new social issues and challenging the status quo to pursuit of compelling narratives.
 My research explores this dual role as a cultural mirror that reflects societal values and a driver of social change that shapes economic behavior.
 My core contribution is the development of novel methods using generative artificial intelligence (AI) to transform unstructured narratives into structured data for rigorous econometric analysis.
 I apply this framework to Latin American telenovelas, whose deep cultural penetration provides an ideal setting to identify the causal effects of media exposure on real-world economic and social outcomes.
 Ultimately, this approach offers a new, high-frequency, and scalable methodology for the empirical study of culture and its influence on human behavior.
 
 #sentence_start()[Job Market Paper]
 In "Does Watching Women Work, Work? The Effect of Television on Female Labor Outcomes", I provide causal evidence that media can be a powerful tool for closing gender gaps.
 To measure the content of these narratives, I developed a novel Female Empowerment Index (FEI) by applying a generative AI model to the synopses of nearly 2,000 telenovelas.
 The FEI is based on an augmented Bechdel-Wallace test and includes questions about the employment status of female characters.
 To overcome endogeneity concerns, I employ an instrumental variable strategy in Mexico that leverages quasi-random variation in television signal loss determined by physical topography.
 The central finding is that a 10% increase in TeenFEI exposure (20 units) leads to a 4% increase in the likelihood of labor market participation (2 p.p.) that accounts for a sizable share of female labor force participation growth in Mexico from 2010 to 2020.
 The effect is driven exclusively by portrayals of women in non-traditional careers and narratives that frame empowerment with positive emotions, demonstrating that how empowerment is portrayed is important.
 
 A second paper, "Drama and Redistribution: The Impact of Telenovelas on Preferences for Redistribution in Latin America", complements this work by showing that telenovelas depicting inequality leads to a counterintuitive decrease in support for redistribution.
 This study's methodological contribution lies in the comparison of multiple Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to identify social class conflict in telenovelas.
 I compared a (i) dictionary-based approach, (ii) a vector embedding model, and (iii) a zero-shot classification using a generative AI model (ChatGPT).
 The ChatGPT approach outperformed the others in accuracy (74%) and precision (80%).
 The analysis reveals that exposure to inequality telenovelas reduces support for redistributive policies by 4%.
 This is because the narratives often misrepresent the experiences of marginalized groups, framing them in ways that downplay systemic issues.
 
 My future research will be dedicated to expanding this methodological pipeline.
 I plan to scale the measurement of cultural values across multiple forms of media worldwide, moving beyond gender and inequality to include other economically relevant values such as community trust, attitudes towards immigration, and environmental protection.
 Such large-scale data assembly and validation naturally create a collaborative environment.
 Building on my experience managing research assistants, this work will provide ideal opportunities for undergraduates to gain hands-on experience, serving as human coders to collect data and benchmark AI model performance.
 
 The most innovative step in this research agenda is to move beyond text, leveraging recent breakthroughs in multimodal generative AI to analyze visual and auditory data directly.
 This expansion will allow the project to include cultural products like movies, books, and music, which transmit essential cultural messages through images and sounds, not just text.
 Thanks to rapidly advancing AI, state-of-the-art multimodal models such as GPT-4o and Google's Gemini can now process text, images, and audio simultaneously.
 Applying these tools will enable a far more complete and nuanced extraction of cultural values from these rich media forms.
 
 Lastly, while my research has so far explored the demand side of media, a compelling future direction is the supply side: how producers of these media types decide which narratives to create and disseminate.
 This new agenda will investigate the extent to which political environments shape media content and whether producers tend to conform to the status quo or push the boundaries of social norms.
 
 My research agenda is driven by a central goal: to show that the narratives in our media have a tangible impact on people's economic lives.
 Ultimately, this work argues that behavioral change isn't only achieved through laws and policies, but by transforming the very stories we tell ourselves.

    JOB ADVERTISEMENT:
    - Tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Economics at Simon Fraser University, starting July 1, 2026 or later  
- Preference for candidates with empirical research in environmental, health, industrial organization, international, or public economics, but all fields considered  
- Must hold (or be near completion of) a PhD in Economics or closely related field  
- Strong record of research productivity and potential for a successful research program  
- Potential for excellence in teaching at undergraduate and graduate levels  
- Expected to contribute to research, teaching, and service within the department and university  
- Commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion is valued  
- Canadian citizens and permanent residents given priority

    INSTITUTION VALUES:
    - Student participation and involvement are central; students are key contributors and decision-makers.
- Responsibility for student experience is shared by the entire SFU community—students, staff, and faculty.
- Student support takes a holistic approach, recognizing the multi-dimensional nature of student identities.
- Customized approaches are valued to address the unique needs of different student sub-groups.
- Collaboration across faculties, service units, and campuses is essential for a seamless student experience.

    ECON DEPARTMENT CONTEXT:
    - The department fosters a dynamic, creative, and globally connected teaching and learning environment.
- Faculty are recognized for high-quality research published in leading economics journals.
- Research strengths include empirical economics, with faculty and students encouraged to develop systematic approaches to economic questions.
- Teaching is led by renowned research professors, supporting both undergraduate and graduate academic excellence.
- Potential for collaboration with faculty such as Chris Bidner, whose work on gender norms aligns with empirical and methodological interests.

    JOB ADVERTISEMENT:
    - Position: Assistant or junior Associate Professor in Economics (applied microeconomics: health, labour, or public economics).
- Start date: July 1, 2026.
- Research: Independent, high-quality research program with peer-reviewed publications and external funding.
- Teaching: Undergraduate and graduate teaching; doctoral supervision.
- Departmental fit: Collegiality, collaboration, and contribution to a positive departmental culture.
- Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion: Strong commitment required; applicants must describe past or planned contributions to EDI.
- Application materials: Letter, CV, research papers (including job market paper), research summary, teaching evidence, three reference letters, and EDI statement.
- Preference: Canadian citizens/permanent residents prioritized.
- Additional: $60,000 special contribution funding; online application via EconJobMarket.

    INSTITUTION VALUES:
    - Commitment to creativity, innovation, and excellence in teaching, research, and scholarship
- Emphasis on integrity, quality, inclusiveness, and teamwork
- Dedication to inclusive excellence and respect for diverse perspectives
- Focus on advancing human and societal health and well-being
- Engagement with local, national, Indigenous, and global communities
- Support for innovation in teaching and learning across disciplines
- Operational excellence to support institutional goals
- Collaboration in world-class research and tackling global issues

    ECON DEPARTMENT CONTEXT:
    - The Department values rigorous training in economic theory and quantitative methods, offering diverse undergraduate and graduate programs.
- Faculty research covers econometrics, experimental, environmental, health, international, labour, macro, micro, population, and public economics, with a strong focus on applied policy issues.
- The Department is recognized as a leading research hub, with affiliations to Canadian research labs and centres.
- Potential co-authors include Katherine Cuff (inequality and redistribution, media effects on attitudes).
- Key research/teaching groups: Mestelman & Muller McMaster Decision Science Laboratory (McDSL), focusing on mechanisms of attitude change with media exposure.

    JOB ADVERTISEMENT:
    - Position: Tenure-track or tenured faculty in Empirical Microeconomics at Waseda University's Faculty of Political Science and Economics, starting April or September 2026.
- Required: Substantial research achievements in applied/empirical microeconomics (any field).
- Required: Ability to teach in both Japanese and English.
- Required: Ph.D. in Economics or a relevant field by the time of appointment.
- Teaching: Courses in microeconomics (undergraduate and graduate), with ability to teach econometrics desirable.
- Teaching experience is desirable.
- Application: Submit cover letter, CV, research and teaching statements, up to three major works, and degree documentation via the online portal.
- Recommendation letters: Two required, with specific submission procedures depending on tenure status.
- Waseda values diversity and inclusion; no discrimination based on sex, disability, sexual orientation, nationality, ethnicity, creed, or age.

    INSTITUTION VALUES:
    - Independence of scholarship: Commitment to academic freedom, critical thinking, and original research.
- Practical application of scholarship: Emphasis on research that benefits society and addresses real-world issues.
- Fostering good citizens: Education aimed at developing individuals who contribute positively to society and the world.
- Legacy of inclusivity and global citizenship: Focus on respecting individuality and promoting global engagement.

    ECON DEPARTMENT CONTEXT:
    - The Faculty integrates undergraduate and graduate education and research, fostering strong collaboration across related schools and institutes.
- Emphasis on interdisciplinary cooperation, with research outcomes feeding back into teaching.
- Faculty members are involved in both practical and academic activities, addressing real-world policy issues and improving academic skills.
- Potential co-authors and research/teaching groups:
    - Patric DEJARNETTE and Elizaveta Kugaevskaia: Behavioral economics, together we can explore the direct on cultural values, attitudes, and behavior in experiments.
    - Shahida PERVIN: Labor economics, with opportunities to explore cultural aspects of labor decisions.

    JOB ADVERTISEMENT:
    - Assistant Professor of Practice in Macroeconomics, with primary teaching in Principles of Macroeconomics.
- Commitment to teaching excellence and demonstrated evidence of effective teaching.
- Ability to teach additional courses in International Trade, Behavioral Economics, and undergraduate Macroeconomic electives.
- Ph.D. in Economics required (ABD considered with completion by Fall 2026).
- Ready to teach in an integrated, cross-disciplinary, and international environment.
- Candidates who bridge theory and practice, bring global perspectives, and highlight economics' role in business are especially welcome.
- Alignment with Babson’s values: teaching and scholarly excellence, collaboration, diversity, and inclusiveness.
- Eagerness to engage with a diverse student community.

    INSTITUTION VALUES:
    MISSION STATEMENT

- Babson College empowers entrepreneurial leaders to create sustainable economic and social value globally.
- Purpose: Enable learners to create lasting value for themselves, their communities, and the world.
- Core values: Integrity, Collaboration, Inclusive Excellence, Innovation, and Excellence.

    ECON DEPARTMENT CONTEXT:
    - The economics department values diversity, rigorous analytical skills, and interactive, hands-on learning.
- Emphasis on applying economic principles to real-world decision making, with a focus on global, socio-economic, and environmental impacts.
- Faculty are dedicated educators and researchers, fostering an environment of collaboration and inclusive excellence.
- Research and teaching topics include entrepreneurship, finance, marketing, management, banking, law, policy, and global economic interactions.
- Potential co-authors and research/teaching groups: Joseph Ricciardi (expertise in Latin America), and a diverse faculty engaged in applied and interdisciplinary economics.

    JOB ADVERTISEMENT:
    - Tenure-track assistant professor position in applied microeconomics, starting July 1, 2026.
- Strong emphasis on empirical causal inference methods.
- Candidates who enhance faculty diversity and support a diverse student body are especially encouraged to apply.
- Must be prepared to teach core courses: Principles of Economics, Intermediate Microeconomics, Statistics, and Econometrics.
- Expected to mentor undergraduate research.
- Demonstrated ability to design inclusive courses and foster an inclusive classroom climate.
- Evidence of active engagement with research is highly valued.
- All Ph.D. requirements must be completed by the appointment date.

    INSTITUTION VALUES:

    BASE RESEARCH STATEMENT:
    Entertainment media (e.g., ttelevision shows, films, books, and music) are more than entertainment products.
 As a group they reflect the cultural norms, identities, and values of the societies that produce them.
 Yet, media can also be a powerful force for change
 A single telenovela can drive cultural change by introducing new social issues and challenging the status quo to pursuit of compelling narratives.
 My research explores this dual role as a cultural mirror that reflects societal values and a driver of social change that shapes economic behavior.
 My core contribution is the development of novel methods using generative artificial intelligence (AI) to transform unstructured narratives into structured data for rigorous econometric analysis.
 I apply this framework to Latin American telenovelas, whose deep cultural penetration provides an ideal setting to identify the causal effects of media exposure on real-world economic and social outcomes.
 Ultimately, this approach offers a new, high-frequency, and scalable methodology for the empirical study of culture and its influence on human behavior.
 
 #sentence_start()[Job Market Paper]
 In "Does Watching Women Work, Work? The Effect of Television on Female Labor Outcomes", I provide causal evidence that media can be a powerful tool for closing gender gaps.
 To measure the content of these narratives, I developed a novel Female Empowerment Index (FEI) by applying a generative AI model to the synopses of nearly 2,000 telenovelas.
 The FEI is based on an augmented Bechdel-Wallace test and includes questions about the employment status of female characters.
 To overcome endogeneity concerns, I employ an instrumental variable strategy in Mexico that leverages quasi-random variation in television signal loss determined by physical topography.
 The central finding is that a 10% increase in TeenFEI exposure (20 units) leads to a 4% increase in the likelihood of labor market participation (2 p.p.) that accounts for a sizable share of female labor force participation growth in Mexico from 2010 to 2020.
 The effect is driven exclusively by portrayals of women in non-traditional careers and narratives that frame empowerment with positive emotions, demonstrating that how empowerment is portrayed is important.
 
 A second paper, "Drama and Redistribution: The Impact of Telenovelas on Preferences for Redistribution in Latin America", complements this work by showing that telenovelas depicting inequality leads to a counterintuitive decrease in support for redistribution.
 This study's methodological contribution lies in the comparison of multiple Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to identify social class conflict in telenovelas.
 I compared a (i) dictionary-based approach, (ii) a vector embedding model, and (iii) a zero-shot classification using a generative AI model (ChatGPT).
 The ChatGPT approach outperformed the others in accuracy (74%) and precision (80%).
 The analysis reveals that exposure to inequality telenovelas reduces support for redistributive policies by 4%.
 This is because the narratives often misrepresent the experiences of marginalized groups, framing them in ways that downplay systemic issues.
 
 My future research will be dedicated to expanding this methodological pipeline.
 I plan to scale the measurement of cultural values across multiple forms of media worldwide, moving beyond gender and inequality to include other economically relevant values such as community trust, attitudes towards immigration, and environmental protection.
 Such large-scale data assembly and validation naturally create a collaborative environment.
 Building on my experience managing research assistants, this work will provide ideal opportunities for undergraduates to gain hands-on experience, serving as human coders to collect data and benchmark AI model performance.
 
 The most innovative step in this research agenda is to move beyond text, leveraging recent breakthroughs in multimodal generative AI to analyze visual and auditory data directly.
 This expansion will allow the project to include cultural products like movies, books, and music, which transmit essential cultural messages through images and sounds, not just text.
 Thanks to rapidly advancing AI, state-of-the-art multimodal models such as GPT-4o and Google's Gemini can now process text, images, and audio simultaneously.
 Applying these tools will enable a far more complete and nuanced extraction of cultural values from these rich media forms.
 
 Lastly, while my research has so far explored the demand side of media, a compelling future direction is the supply side: how producers of these media types decide which narratives to create and disseminate.
 This new agenda will investigate the extent to which political environments shape media content and whether producers tend to conform to the status quo or push the boundaries of social norms.
 
 My research agenda is driven by a central goal: to show that the narratives in our media have a tangible impact on people's economic lives.
 Ultimately, this work argues that behavioral change isn't only achieved through laws and policies, but by transforming the very stories we tell ourselves.
